title: The Hollowed Center: Why Larijani's Death Changes Everything for Iran
summary: The confirmation of Ali Larijani's death on March 17, following a targeted Israeli air strike at his daughter's home in Pardis, isn't just another casualty—it's the removal of the Islamic Republic's 'brain trust' and leaves Iran without its most strategic player.
content:
In the high-stakes conflict currently unfolding, Iran just lost its most strategic player. The confirmation of Ali Larijani's death on March 17, following a targeted Israeli air strike at his daughter's home in Pardis, isn't just another casualty—it's the removal of the Islamic Republic's 'brain trust.'

Unlike the generals who have been targeted in recent weeks, Larijani wasn't a man of the barracks; he was a man of the boardroom. As the Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, he was the rare bridge between rigid ideology and cold, hard pragmatism. Following the assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei on February 28, Larijani had emerged as the de facto strategist, the one man capable of managing the 'triple threat' currently suffocating Tehran: a regional war, a domestic uprising met with brutal crackdowns, and a nuclear program under literal fire.

Why his absence creates a vacuum:

The Loss of the 'Operator': Larijani was a survivor who had run state broadcasting, served as Speaker of Parliament for over a decade, and acted as a chief nuclear negotiator. He knew how the gears of the state turned in a way few others did.

A Fragile Succession: His death leaves the new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, without one of his father's most trusted and experienced advisors at a time when the leadership is increasingly forced into the shadows for security.

The Shift to the Military: With the 'pragmatic' civilian-political wing losing its heavyweights, power is drifting toward a fractured military leadership. As President Masoud Pezeshkian recently hinted, armed forces units may now be operating with broader autonomy. In practice, this means decisions may be taken more quickly, but with far less central coordination.

We aren't just looking at a country at war; we're looking at a state struggling to maintain its own architecture. With Larijani gone, the 'calculated strategy' he championed is likely to be replaced by a more volatile, reactionary posture—both in the regional conflict and in the repression of dissent at home.

title: At Least 100 Killed in Pakistani Air Strike on Kabul Drug Rehab Centre
summary: More than 100 people have been killed after a Pakistani air strike leveled a major drug treatment facility in the Afghan capital, according to forensic sources. The strike targeted the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital, a sprawling complex that was once a US military base.
content:
KABUL — More than 100 people have been killed after a Pakistani air strike leveled a major drug treatment facility in the Afghan capital, according to forensic sources.

The strike, which occurred late Monday night, targeted the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital, a sprawling complex that was once a US military base. While forensic experts in Kabul say many victims are charred beyond recognition, Taliban officials claim the death toll could be as high as 400.

'Military Installations' vs. Civilian Care

Pakistan's Ministry of Information has denied intentionally striking the hospital, maintaining that its forces 'precisely targeted military installations and terrorist support infrastructure.' Islamabad argues that Afghanistan continues to harbor militants responsible for cross-border terrorism—a claim Kabul has repeatedly dismissed.

However, Afghan health ministry spokesman Sharafat Zaman Amarkhail told the BBC there were no military targets in the vicinity of the rehab center. 'This was a place of healing, not a base for combat,' he stated.

The Scene in Kabul

Residents reported massive explosions rocking the capital at approximately 20:50 local time, followed by the roar of jet engines and the activation of air defense batteries. By Tuesday morning, the scale of the devastation was clear:

Flattened Infrastructure: The center, which housed roughly 2,000 patients, has been reduced to rubble.

Desperate Searches: Families gathered outside the perimeter Tuesday morning, searching through debris littered with blankets and shoes for any sign of survivors.

Casualty Reports: While laboratory sources confirmed 100 deaths, BBC reporters on the ground witnessed dozens of bodies being removed on stretchers shortly after the blast.

International Outcry

The UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA) has condemned the attack and called for an immediate ceasefire. 'UNAMA reiterates its call for de-escalation... and for parties to comply with their obligations under international law to ensure the protection of civilians,' the agency said in a statement.

China has also stepped in to mediate, with Foreign Minister Wang Yi reportedly holding emergency calls with both counterparts in Kabul and Islamabad. Beijing has urged both nations to 'remain calm and exercise restraint' to prevent a full-scale regional war.

A Pattern of Escalation

The strike marks a bloody turning point in a conflict that re-ignited last month. According to UN data, at least 75 people were killed in cross-border skirmishes between February 26 and March 13 alone. Despite a short-lived ceasefire in October, the border remains one of the most volatile flashpoints in Central Asia.

The Omid center was a centerpiece of the Taliban's efforts to address the city's drug crisis since their return to power in 2021. Now, it stands as a charred reminder of a conflict that shows no signs of cooling.

title: Israel Claims Assassination of Iran's Security Chief Larijani and Basij Commander
summary: In a major escalation on Day 18 of the conflict, Israel claims to have killed Ali Larijani and Gholamreza Soleimani. Tehran has not confirmed, and a handwritten note from Larijani has raised questions about proof of life.
content:
In a major escalation on Day 18 of the US-Israel war on Iran, Israel has claimed the assassination of two of Iran's most influential figures: Ali Larijani, Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council, and Gholamreza Soleimani, the commander of the internal Basij militia.

While Israeli Defense Minister Yoav Katz hailed the operations as a termination of the regime's leadership capabilities, Tehran has yet to officially confirm the deaths.

The Shadow of Larijani

If confirmed, Ali Larijani would be the highest-ranking official killed since the devastating US-Israeli strikes on the first day of the war, which claimed the life of former Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.

Larijani is a titan of Iranian politics. A former Speaker of Parliament and a veteran nuclear negotiator, he was seen just days ago alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian at an al-Quds Day rally.

The 'Proof of Life' Mystery

Shortly after the Israeli announcement, Iranian state media circulated a handwritten note purportedly from Larijani. The note honors 84 sailors killed in a recent US attack, but analysts remain skeptical, noting it is unclear if the message was pre-recorded or truly a proof of life.

Targeting the Internal Guard: Gholamreza Soleimani

The Israeli military also announced a targeted strike in the heart of Tehran that allegedly eliminated Gholamreza Soleimani. As the head of the Basij — a massive volunteer paramilitary force under the IRGC — Soleimani was the backbone of Iran's internal security and dissent suppression.

"Our army is working with strength to continue to hit and terminate missile capabilities as well as strategic infrastructure," Minister Katz posted on social media.

What This Means for the War

Despite the symbolic weight of these claims, experts warn against assuming a total collapse of the Iranian hierarchy.

A 'Whack-a-Mole' Strategy: Analysts suggest that while these strikes are psychologically damaging, the Iranian system is designed for continuity, with deputies ready to step into vacated roles immediately.

Regional Chaos: The fallout is already being felt beyond Iran's borders. From Iraq, where sports events are being delayed due to travel chaos, to the UAE, where drone strikes have ignited fires at major oil hubs, the US-Israel war on Iran is rapidly becoming a global crisis.

title: Trump's confidence is undimmed — but every Iran option comes with risk
summary: The president is acting like business as usual, but rising gas prices and reluctant allies are making the Iran conflict a political headache that won't go away.
content:
For Donald Trump, the third week of the conflict with Iran looks a lot like any other week. On Monday, he held court at the White House for over an hour, touching on everything from the war effort to Kennedy Center renovations. Over the weekend, he was on the golf course in Florida.

On the surface, the man is unbothered. He's posting on Truth Social, railing against the Supreme Court one minute and declaring the war 'already won' the next. But beneath that familiar exterior, a more complicated truth is brewing — every American president eventually learns that you might be able to ignore a war, but a war will not ignore you.

The White House was forced to delay a planned presidential trip to China because of the conflict. What was supposed to be a swift campaign now has a timeline that stretches 'for weeks or even longer,' according to military briefings.

The ally problem

Trump's weekend call for a global coalition to secure the Strait of Hormuz — a vital waterway for the world's oil — has been met with a collective shrug from key allies.

The UK, Japan, Australia and several European powers have politely declined to join a new military coalition. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer made it clear: 'We will not be drawn into a wider war.'

This leaves Trump in a political bind. If he goes all in and uses more of the US Navy to secure the strait, American forces are put directly in harm's way. If he pulls back, Iran could continue to threaten shipping, keeping oil prices high.

'It's a little unfair,' Trump said Monday. 'You win a war, but they have no right to be doing what they're doing.'

The political math: Gas prices and 'forever wars'

For the average American, the geopolitics of the Strait of Hormuz might feel distant. The price at the pump does not.

The average cost for a gallon of gas has shot up to $3.72 — a massive jump from $2.94 just a month ago. That number is the one that really matters in a presidential election year.

'It just blows up everything,' said Clifford Young, a political analyst with Ipsos. For a president who ran on fixing the economy, a gas price spike is the 'quickest way to lose the middle.'

Trump's base is holding firm for now, but the erosion is happening at the edges — with moderate Republicans and independents. If the war drags on and prices stay high, the 'affordability agenda' that defines his domestic policy starts to sink.

The 'boots on the ground' nightmare

The biggest risk for the administration is escalation. Reports that a 5,000-strong Marine unit is being moved from Japan to the Middle East suggest the military is preparing for more than just airstrikes.

Putting troops on the ground changes everything. It turns a distant campaign into a potential quagmire. Americans are suffering from what Young calls 'forever war fatigue.' The memory of Afghanistan and Iraq is still fresh, and many voters who backed Trump did so because he promised to stay out of new ones.

'If we put boots on the ground, that's a whole new risk for the administration,' Young said. 'That changes everything.'

What happens now?

Trump is currently juggling two very different realities. In public, he remains the confident negotiator, insisting, 'We're the strongest nation in the world.'

But privately, the math is getting harder. He can escalate and risk American lives and a political backlash. He can hold steady and hope allies come around. Or he can declare victory and go home, risking that Iran remains a threat.

The midterm elections are still seven months away — an eternity in politics, but barely enough time to solve a Middle East crisis.

For now, Trump is betting that his brand of confidence will outlast the chaos. But as every president before him has learned, wars have a habit of writing their own endings.
